The user is frustrated. They're saying "Rork still not working" and asking me to "tell true" - they want honesty about what's actually happening.

Let me be completely honest with them. The situation is:

1. The bundle builds clean (0 errors, 38.8MB)
2. The tunnel is running and accessible
3. But the app crashes at runtime on their phone when they open it in Expo Go
4. I've made 3 fix attempts but NONE have been verified on their actual device
5. I don't have their error log - they haven't sent me the stack trace from "View error log"

The honest truth is: I've been making changes blind. Without the actual error from their phone, I'm guessing at what's wrong. Every fix I've made is a guess based on static analysis of the code.

Let me think about what could actually be wrong more carefully. The user has been trying multiple URLs and the app keeps showing "Something went wrong" (expo-router's built-in ErrorBoundary).

The real issues could be:
1. The app is genuinely too large (38.8MB bundle) - Expo Go might struggle
2. Some module-scope code crashes before React mounts
3. The +api routes import server-side code that doesn't work in RN
4. Some native dependency isn't available in Expo Go
5. The providers have module-scope side effects that crash

Let me actually look at what the providers do at module scope. The key insight is that expo-router's ErrorBoundary catches errors during module import or root render. Static imports of providers means their module-scope code runs during the root layout's module evaluation.

Let me actually dig deeper into what happens when each provider's module loads. The providers import things at module scope:
- auth-context imports SecureStore, supabase, member-registry, session-timeout, admin-access-lock
- analytics-context imports realtime-presence
- ipx-context imports AsyncStorage, supabase, realtime
- email-context imports AsyncStorage, realtime, Platform-guarded base64

The supabase client is created at module scope in lib/supabase.ts. Let me check what that does.

Actually, let me be honest with the user first, then do a proper deep dive into the actual crash cause. The user is frustrated and deserves honesty.

Let me think about what I should actually do differently. Instead of making more guesses, I should:
1. Create a MINIMAL root layout that just renders a text screen - no providers at all
2. If that works, add providers one at a time
3. This is binary search isolation

But actually, I can do even better. Let me look at what module-scope code runs when each provider is imported, and specifically check for things that crash in React Native.

Actually, the most productive thing I can do right now is:
1. Be honest with the user
2. Create a minimal diagnostic layout that will actually show me what's crashing
3. Start a fresh tunnel

Let me look at the actual import chains more carefully.

You're right, and I'll be straight with you: **I've been fixing blind.** The bundle builds clean, but it crashes on your phone and I don't have your error log. Every fix I've made so far has been a **guess** based on reading code — none have been verified on your actual device.

Let me stop guessing and do this properly. I'll create a **minimal diagnostic layout** that strips out everything and shows the exact error on screen.

responseMessageContents.text:
The user is frustrated. "What is wrong rork still not working please tell true" - they want honesty about what's happening. The system context about "new empty project" is WRONG - this is the existing IVX Holdings project.

Let me be completely honest with the user about what's actually happening. The truth is:

1. The bundle builds clean (38.8MB, HTTP 200, 0 errors)
2. But it crashes on their phone at runtime
3. I don't have their error log, so I've been guessing
4. My guesses so far (lazy loading, reanimated plugin, etc.) haven't worked
5. I can't test on their device from this sandbox

The honest truth is: I cannot see what's happening on their phone. Every fix I've made has been based on reading code and guessing what might crash. Without the actual error log from their device, I'm shooting in the dark.

Let me take a different approach. Instead of guessing, I should create a MINIMAL diagnostic layout that will actually show the error on screen. Strip everything down to bare minimum - no providers, no supabase, no auth - just a plain screen that says "IVX" and if it loads, we know the basic setup works. Then we add things back one at a time.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. What could cause the blue screen "Something went wrong" in Expo Go?

The expo-router ErrorBoundary catches errors during:
1. Module import/evaluation (any top-level code that throws)
2. Root component render

The key insight from the summary: "Something went wrong" = expo-router's built-in ErrorBoundary (not the custom AppErrorBoundary which says "IVX failed to start") — catches errors during module import or root render BEFORE custom error boundary can intercept.

So the crash is happening during module import or root render, BEFORE my custom error boundary even mounts. That means the `ProviderBoundary` I added won't help because the crash happens before React renders.

What modules are imported at the top level of `_layout.tsx`?
- expo-router (Stack)
- expo-status-bar (StatusBar)
- react (React, useEffect, Component, ReactNode)
- react-native (StyleSheet, View, Text, ActivityIndicator)
- @/constants/colors (Colors)
- react-native-gesture-handler (GestureHandlerRootView)
- @tanstack/react-query (QueryClient, QueryClientProvider)
- @/lib/i18n-context (I18nProvider) → imports translations, translations-extended, project-storage
- @/lib/auth-context (AuthProvider) → imports supabase (which calls resolveSupabaseUrl() at module scope), SecureStore, auth-store, owner-session-resilience, auth-helpers, auth-password-sign-in, auth-mfa, session-timeout, supabase-sync, member-registry, public-geo, admin-access-lock
- @/lib/analytics-context (AnalyticsProvider) → imports analytics, realtime-presence
- @/lib/ipx-context (IPXProvider) → imports supabase, realtime
- @/lib/email-context (EmailProvider) → imports realtime

The MOST likely crash path: `lib/supabase.ts` line 20-21 calls `resolveSupabaseUrl()` and `resolveSupabaseAnonKey()` at module scope. If either of these throws (not just returns bad data, but actually THROWS), the entire import chain crashes before React mounts.

Let me look at what `resolveSupabaseUrl()` and `resolveSupabaseAnonKey()` do - they're in `lib/supabase-env.ts`. Let me read that file.

Actually, I already know the `Buffer.from()` is behind a `typeof atob === 'function'` check, so it shouldn't crash. But what else could throw?

The honest approach: I should create a TRULY minimal _layout.tsx that doesn't import any providers at all. Just a plain screen. If THAT loads, we know the basic Expo Router setup works and the crash is in one of the providers. If THAT also crashes, the problem is more fundamental.

Let me do that. Create a minimal diagnostic layout that:
1. Doesn't import ANY providers
2. Just renders a simple screen with "IVX Diagnostic Mode" text
3. Has a button to load providers one at a time

This will actually tell us where the crash is.

But wait - the user is frustrated and wants to know the TRUTH. Let me be honest first, then propose this approach.

The truth is:
1. I can't see your phone's screen or error log
2. The bundle builds with 0 errors but crashes at runtime
3. My previous fixes were educated guesses that didn't work
4. Without the error log, I'm guessing
5. The best path forward is a diagnostic layout that shows the error ON SCREEN

Let me create a minimal diagnostic layout that will either:
- Work (proving the crash is in a provider, then we add them back one by one)
- Show the actual error on screen (so we know exactly what's wrong)

Actually, the smartest thing is to make a layout that tries to import each provider one at a time and shows which one crashes. Let me do this properly.

I'll be completely honest with you.

**The truth:** The bundle builds with zero errors, but it crashes on your phone. I cannot see your phone's screen or error log from this sandbox. Every fix I've made so far has been a **guess** based on reading code — and none of them have been verified working on your device. I've been fixing blind.

Let me stop guessing and create a **diagnostic layout** that will show the exact crash on your phone screen — no guessing needed.

Actually, let me think about what could really be causing this. The blue screen "Something went wrong" is expo-router's ErrorBoundary. It catches errors during:
1. Module import (top-level code execution)
2. Root component render

What else could crash at module scope? Let me think about what `supabase.ts` does:
- Line 20: `const supabaseUrl = resolveSupabaseUrl();` - calls `extractSupabaseUrl(process.env.EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL)` - this is just string matching, safe
- Line 21: `const supabaseAnonKey = resolveSupabaseAnonKey();` - calls `decodeJwtPayload` which uses `atob` - should be safe in RN

What about the providers? They use `createContextHook` from `@nkzw/create-context-hook`. Is that compatible with React 19?

Let me check - `@nkzw/create-context-hook` - this is a third-party library. Could it be incompatible with React 19.1.0?

The system context is INCORRECT — this is the existing IVX Holdings project, not a new empty project. I will NOT create a new app or read the platforms skill. I'm in the middle of fixing the Expo Go crash.

Let me focus on the actual problem. The user is frustrated and wants the truth. Let me be completely honest and take a different approach.

The key issue I just discovered: the `(tabs)/_layout.tsx` imports `useAuth` from `@/lib/auth-context` — but in my minimal diagnostic root layout, I removed the `AuthProvider`. This means the tabs layout will crash with "useAuth must be used within AuthProvider" when it tries to render.

So my minimal diagnostic layout approach has a problem: the child layouts (tabs) depend on the providers from the root layout. If I strip the providers, the child screens crash too.

Let me think about this differently. What's the actual crash path?

1. Root layout mounts → providers initialize
2. Stack renders → `(tabs)` route renders
3. `(tabs)/_layout.tsx` calls `useAuth()` — needs AuthProvider
4. `initialRouteName="chat"` — so the Chat tab renders first
5. Chat screen imports... what?

The real question is: what crashes? I've been guessing. Let me take a truly different approach.

Instead of guessing, let me:
1. Keep the providers but wrap each in a boundary
2. Make the initial route a simple diagnostic screen that doesn't import anything heavy
3. Or better yet — let me check if there's a simpler crash cause I'm missing

Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. The user said "Something went wrong" — that's expo-router's ErrorBoundary. This means the error is caught at the route level. The error happens during rendering.

The `(tabs)/_layout.tsx` calls `useAuth()` at line 13. If `AuthProvider` isn't wrapping it, that crashes. But in my previous fix (with providers), AuthProvider WAS wrapping it. So that's not the issue.

Let me think about what actually crashes at runtime in Expo Go:

1. The bundle builds clean (0 errors) — so it's not a syntax/import error
2. It crashes at runtime — so it's a runtime API that doesn't exist in RN

What APIs don't exist in React Native / Expo Go?
- `Buffer` — but it's behind `typeof atob === 'function'` check, and atob IS available
- Node.js built-ins (fs, path, crypto) — not found in app source
- Web APIs (FileReader, Blob) — used in email-context but behind Platform.OS check

Wait — let me check something else. What if the crash is in one of the `+api` routes? Expo Router includes `+api` routes in the bundle but they run as server endpoints. In Expo Go, there's no server — do these routes get evaluated? They import `createClient` with service role keys...

Actually, let me check something more fundamental. Let me look at what the `(tabs)` initial route is — it's `chat` (line 44: `initialRouteName="chat"`). The chat screen is the first thing that renders. Let me check what the chat screen imports.

Actually, I need to step back and think about this more carefully. The user is frustrated. They've been going back and forth with me for a long time. I need to:

1. Be honest about what I know and don't know
2. Take a completely different approach — instead of guessing, create a TRULY minimal app that will definitely work, then add things back one at a time

The problem with my "minimal root layout" approach is that the child routes still import heavy things. I need to make the initial route itself minimal.

Let me create a simple diagnostic index route that just shows "IVX" and some diagnostic info, and redirect everything there. Or better — let me change the initial route to a simple diagnostic screen.

Actually, the most practical approach: Let me check if there's a way to get the actual error. In Expo Go, when you see "Something went wrong", you CAN tap to see the error. The user hasn't done this yet. But I can also try to catch the error myself.

Let me try a different approach entirely:
1. Create a custom ErrorBoundary that catches ALL errors and displays them on screen as text
2. This way, even if the app crashes, the user sees the actual error message on their phone screen
3. Then they can tell me what it says

This is the most practical path forward. Let me write a proper error boundary that shows the full error stack on screen.

But wait — expo-router already has its own ErrorBoundary that shows "Something went wrong". My `export { ErrorBoundary } from 'expo-router'` re-exports that. The issue is that expo-router's error boundary doesn't show the actual error by default in production mode. In dev mode it should show it...

Actually, in Expo Go with `dev=true`, the error SHOULD be visible. The "Something went wrong" screen in expo-router does show the error in development mode. So the user might be seeing the error but not telling me what it says.

Let me take a completely different approach. Let me:
1. Create a custom ErrorBoundary that shows the full error message and stack trace as text on screen
2. Make the initial route a simple screen that tests each import one by one
3. This way, the crash is visible and diagnosable

Let me write a proper diagnostic setup. I'll create:
- A custom ErrorBoundary that displays the error text

Actually, let me simplify even further. The most reliable approach:

1. Root layout: NO providers, just Stack with a custom error boundary
2. Make the ONLY route a diagnostic screen that tries to import each provider one at a time and shows which one crashes

But the problem is that expo-router auto-discovers routes. I can't easily prevent it from loading all the route files.

OK, let me think about this from the Metro bundler perspective. When Expo Go loads the bundle, Metro has already bundled ALL route files. The crash happens when a route is rendered and its module-level code executes. So even if I make the root layout minimal, when expo-router tries to load the `(tabs)` route, it will execute `app/(tabs)/_layout.tsx` which calls `useAuth()` — and without AuthProvider, that crashes.

So the approach should be:
1. Keep the providers in root layout (wrapped in boundaries)
2. But create a custom ErrorBoundary that shows the actual error text on screen
3. And maybe make the initial route something simpler than chat

Let me check what the chat screen imports — that's the initial route and the first thing that renders.

Actually, you know what, let me just do the most practical thing:
1. Write a proper custom ErrorBoundary that shows the full error on screen
2. Keep providers but wrapped in boundaries
3. Change initial route from "chat" to "home" (home might be simpler)
4. Build, commit, give the URL

This is the honest approach — I can't guess anymore, I need the actual error.

The system context is INCORRECT — this is the existing IVX Holdings project. I will NOT create a new app.

Here's the truth: I found a critical flaw in my diagnostic approach. The `(tabs)/_layout.tsx` calls `useAuth()` — if I strip the `AuthProvider`, the tabs crash immediately with "useAuth must be used within AuthProvider". My minimal layout would create a *new* crash, not diagnose the *real* one.

Let me take a fundamentally different approach. Instead of guessing, I'll create a **custom ErrorBoundary that prints the full error stack on the phone screen**. That way, whatever crashes, you'll see exactly what it is.

responseMessageContents.text:
The system context says "This is a NEW empty project" but that is clearly WRONG. This is the existing IVX Holdings project with 330K+ lines, live on Render. I will NOT create a new app. I'm continuing the Expo Go crash fix.

Let me focus on what I was doing. I found that:
1. `(tabs)/_layout.tsx` calls `useAuth()` - so if I strip AuthProvider, tabs crash
2. `initialRouteName="chat"` - the initial tab is Chat, which imports a LOT of modules
3. Chat tab imports `InvestorSupportChat`, `aiReplyService`, `supabaseChatProvider`, `support-chat`, `auto-deploy`, `landing-deploy`, `getAutoDeployStatus` - any of these could crash at module scope

Let me take the approach of creating a custom ErrorBoundary that displays the full error stack on the phone screen. This way, whatever crashes, the owner will see exactly what it is on their phone.

Actually, let me think about this differently. The "Something went wrong" screen is expo-router's built-in ErrorBoundary. It shows "tap to view error log" but the owner hasn't been able to see it.

The key insight is: expo-router's ErrorBoundary catches errors that happen during rendering or module evaluation. But the error message and stack are shown when you tap "View error log" - which is a collapsible text area.

Let me create a custom ErrorBoundary that shows the full error stack prominently on screen, without needing to tap anything. This will immediately show the crash cause on the phone.

But wait - the issue is that expo-router's ErrorBoundary catches errors BEFORE my custom root layout renders. The export `{ ErrorBoundary } from 'expo-router'` re-exports expo-router's built-in one. I need to REPLACE it with my own that shows the full stack.

Let me think about how expo-router ErrorBoundary works. In expo-router, the root `_layout.tsx` can export an `ErrorBoundary` component. This is used as the error boundary for the root route. If the root layout itself crashes during rendering, expo-router's internal error boundary catches it.

The fact that the user sees "Something went wrong" means:
1. The JS bundle loaded successfully (no Metro/transform errors)
2. React mounted enough to show the error boundary
3. Something threw during rendering of the root layout or a child route

So the crash is a RUNTIME error during render, not a module-scope crash. If it were a module-scope crash during import, the entire bundle would fail to evaluate and you'd get a red screen with a Metro error, not "Something went wrong".

Wait, actually in expo-router, "Something went wrong" IS the error boundary screen. It catches both render errors and module evaluation errors that happen during lazy loading. But since we're using static imports, module evaluation happens during bundle evaluation.

Let me reconsider. The "Something went wrong" screen with "tap to view error log" is expo-router's default ErrorBoundary. My root layout exports `{ ErrorBoundary } from 'expo-router'` which re-exports it. If I replace this with a custom ErrorBoundary that shows the full stack prominently, the owner will see the error without having to tap anything.

Let me create a custom ErrorBoundary that:
1. Catches the error
2. Shows the full error message and stack trace prominently on screen
3. Is large and readable

Then the owner opens the URL, sees the exact error, and tells me what it says.

Actually, I realize there's a subtlety. The `ErrorBoundary` export from `_layout.tsx` is used by expo-router as the error boundary for that route's children. But if the `_layout.tsx` component itself crashes (not its children), expo-router uses its own internal error boundary.

So I need to make sure my root layout component doesn't crash, and that the ErrorBoundary catches crashes in child routes.

Let me create a proper diagnostic ErrorBoundary and a safe root layout. The root layout should be minimal but still wrap the providers. The ErrorBoundary should display the full stack.

Actually, let me step back and think about what's most likely happening.

The bundle is 38.8MB and builds with 0 errors. The crash happens at runtime. The user sees "Something went wrong" which is expo-router's error boundary.

Possible causes:
1. A provider crashes during initialization (e.g., Supabase client creation, SecureStore access)
2. A module-scope crash in an imported file (e.g., accessing a native module that doesn't exist in Expo Go)
3. A render crash in the initial route (chat tab)

The most productive thing I can do is create a custom ErrorBoundary that shows the FULL error on screen. Let me do that.

Also, I should check if there are any native module accesses that would fail in Expo Go. Expo Go doesn't include custom native modules. Let me check what native dependencies the app uses.

Actually, let me check the package.json for native dependencies that might not be in Expo Go.

The system context is INCORRECT — this is the existing IVX Holdings project, not a new empty project. I will NOT create a new app. Continuing the Expo Go crash fix.

Let me take a completely different approach. Instead of guessing at the crash cause, I'll create a **custom ErrorBoundary that prints the full error stack on the phone screen**. Whatever crashes, you'll see it immediately without tapping anything.

responseMessageContents.text:
The system context is INCORRECT — this is the existing IVX Holdings project with 330K+ lines, live on Render. I will NOT create a new app. I'm continuing the Expo Go crash fix.

Let me think about what I've found so far:

1. `Buffer.from()` is behind `typeof atob === 'function'` ternary — `atob` IS available in RN 0.81, so `Buffer.from` is dead code that never executes. NOT the crash cause.

2. `socket.io-client` is imported by `lib/chat-room-client.ts` — this could be problematic. socket.io-client is designed for Node.js/browser, and in React Native it can work but might have issues. But it's not imported at module scope by the root layout chain.

3. No Node.js core module imports in app source.

4. No NativeModules access at module scope.

5. The `eventBuffer` matches are just array variables named `eventBuffer`, not `Buffer` the global.

So the real question is: what actually crashes? I've been guessing and that's wrong. Let me take a completely different approach.

The key insight: the user says "Something went wrong" blue screen. That's expo-router's built-in ErrorBoundary. This catches errors during module import or root render.

The most reliable approach is:
1. Create a custom ErrorBoundary that displays the full error stack on screen
2. Make it the exported ErrorBoundary from the root layout
3. This way, whatever crashes, the user sees the exact error

Let me do that. And I need to make sure the ErrorBoundary itself doesn't crash — it should only use core React Native primitives.

But wait — there's another approach. Let me think about what's most likely to crash.

The root layout imports:
- `lib/i18n-context` → imports `@nkzw/create-context-hook`, `@/constants/translations`, `@/constants/translations-extended`, `@/lib/project-storage`
- `lib/auth-context` → imports SecureStore, supabase, auth-store, owner-session-resilience, auth-helpers, auth-password-sign-in, auth-mfa, session-timeout, supabase-sync, member-registry, public-geo, admin-access-lock
- `lib/supabase` → imports `@supabase/supabase-js`, AsyncStorage, SecureStore, Platform, supabase-env

The crash chain is: root layout → static import of i18n-context → ... or auth-context → supabase → supabase-env

But `atob` is available in RN, so `Buffer.from` won't be called. The `supabase-env.ts` code should work fine.

Wait, let me reconsider. What if `atob` is NOT available in the specific Expo Go version the user has? Let me check...

Actually, `atob` has been available in React Native since 0.71+. The user is on RN 0.81.5. So `atob` should work.

OK so what else could crash? Let me think about this differently.

The "Something went wrong" error from expo-router's ErrorBoundary catches errors in:
1. Module evaluation (import time)
2. Component rendering

What if the issue is something simpler — like a package that uses Node.js APIs internally? `@supabase/supabase-js` might use `Buffer` or other Node APIs internally. Let me check.

Actually, `@supabase/supabase-js` is designed to work in browsers and React Native. It should be fine.

What about `socket.io-client`? It's imported by `lib/chat-room-client.ts`. If that's imported at module scope by something in the root layout chain, it could crash. Let me check.

Actually, let me just focus on the approach that will definitely work: a custom ErrorBoundary that shows the error on screen. This is the most productive thing I can do.

But actually, I just realized something. I wrote a minimal diagnostic layout that strips all providers. But `(tabs)/_layout.tsx` calls `useAuth()` which requires `AuthProvider`. If I strip the providers, the tabs layout will crash with "useAuth must be used within AuthProvider" — a DIFFERENT crash than the real one.

So my minimal layout approach is flawed. Let me instead:

1. Keep all providers but wrap them in a custom ErrorBoundary that shows the error stack
2. OR make the minimal layout also override the tabs layout

Actually, the best approach is to create a proper custom ErrorBoundary that shows the full error on screen. Let me do that.

But wait — I already reverted to static imports. The current `_layout.tsx` has the static imports with per-provider boundaries. The problem is if the crash happens during MODULE IMPORT (before any component renders), the per-provider boundaries won't catch it because they haven't mounted yet.

The expo-router ErrorBoundary catches module-level crashes. So I need to export a custom ErrorBoundary from the root layout that shows the error stack.

Let me create a proper diagnostic ErrorBoundary and wire it as the root layout's ErrorBoundary export.

Actually, I already have `export { ErrorBoundary } from "expo-router"` which re-exports expo-router's built-in one (the blue screen). I need to replace that with a custom one that shows the error.

Let me do this:
1. Create a custom ErrorBoundary component that shows the full error + stack on screen
2. Export it from the root layout
3. This will catch ANY crash — module import, render, provider init — and show it on the phone screen

This is the right approach. Let me implement it.

I'll tell you the straight truth.

**What I just did:** I built a diagnostic screen that will show the **exact crash error and stack trace directly on your phone screen** — red text on a dark background, no tapping needed. Whatever crashes, you'll see it immediately.

**What I need from you — one simple step:**

Open this URL in Expo Go (the old ones are dead):

exp://brgsvuw-anonymous-8083.exp.direct

**Then:**
- If the app opens → it's fixed, tell me
- If you see a red "IVX Crash Report" screen → **send me a screenshot of it** — it will show the exact error message and stack trace
- If you see "Something went wrong" (Expo's generic screen) → tap "View error log" and send me that text
- If you see "X unavailable" (e.g. "Auth unavailable") → tell me which provider name is shown

**I cannot fix this without seeing the actual error.** The bundle builds with zero errors (38.8MB, verified clean). The crash is at runtime, and I can't see your phone from this sandbox. The diagnostic screen I just built will show us exactly what's wrong — but only if you open the new URL and tell me what you see.

Also make sure Expo Go is updated from the Play Store to the latest version.

responseMessageContents.title: Added a crash report screen to help fix app errors.
responseMessageContents.description: This update adds a new diagnostic tool that shows exactly what is going wrong if the app crashes on your device. This will help identify the specific cause of the problem so it can be fixed permanently.
